evidence_quotes.quote: inoperable because of the necessity of, for instance, online authentication, on servers that no longer exist, or due to anti-tamper or anti-cheat software that no longer serves it's purpose.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I would like to ask you to please include making game software inoperable or just abandoning it "as is" by manufacturers to the list of things that consumers should be protected from. In stead I would like to see that discontinued games be given to the consumer (owner) in a state that is playable or in such a form that they themselves are able to find ways to legitimately continue using it. I have played computer games for most of my life (at least 40 years). And so I have bought many, many games. Most of these I still have in my possession on the original CD-rom or DVD-rom or are owned in digital form on my hard drives. Many of the older ones are however inoperable because of the necessity of, for instance, online authentication, on servers that no longer exist, or due to anti-tamper or anti-cheat software that no longer serves it's purpose. Let's take Electronic Arts' "Mercenaries 2: world in flames" a game I bought many, many years ago at our local toy-store as an example. Without the authentication demand this game would still play as it did when I bought it. (Perhaps with some software to help it communicate to a more modern system. but still very much functioning.) The manufacturer could have simply made this requirement obsolete in the code (via an update or patch file or even better a complete file of the game free of any restrictions) and I would still be able to play my game. Right now. I can, with some effort, actually still find this game, and some like it, online in a fan community driven reverse engineered version, which is I believe legally somewhat questionable. But why should I? As I legitimately bought this in a store?! Not only that, but why is there no protection in place to prevent the destruction of this art form. And, while in the eye of the beholder, I do genuinely see it as such. I have never had a book publisher come to my house and put a lock on my bookcase once they felt like it, nor a gallery owner blacking out a painting I bought. So why is this just allowed, and has it gone unquestioned for so long? If fairness is what this act aims for, then please consider how fair this treatment of the consumers of this less tangible art form is. Thank you very much for your time and efforts. Yours sincerely. AS
